definition: Ever-rising spirit; optimism as discipline rather than mood. In the watershed it names the entire civilisational project: the refusal to let catastrophe have the last word, renewed daily, like a kitchen, like a garden, like dawn.

The welfare of all — the closing line of the ardaas, taken in this age as an engineering requirement. A system that serves only its builders is considered unfinished. The legal doctrine of 'everyone downstream, forever' descends directly from this phrase.

The federated mesh of community kitchens, solar roofs, and grain stores that forms the watershed's food and power utility. Built on the langar floor's ancient protocol: same level, same meal, no questions about deserving.

Sun-service: the tending of the solar commons as a form of seva. Polishing mirrors, walking the canopy lines, folding the panels before dust storms. Malwa's term, now universal. The sun gives without being asked; dhup-seva is the answering courtesy.

Water assembly: the gathering, open to all residents of a watershed reach, that governs its water. Descends from both the village panchayat and the sangat of the gurdwara. Decisions require the assent of a designated 'voice of the unborn' — usually the youngest person willing to sit through the whole meeting.

The ambrosial hour before dawn, traditionally kept for meditation. The Crescent organises its entire working day around it; metaphorically, the chroniclers use it for the present age — the world's long night ended, the heat of day not yet come, everything still possible.

To share before consuming; one of the three pillars of Sikh practice. In the Graft Country it is extended to genetics: no seed variety may be owned, every graft is lent forward. Abundance understood as a technique whose first step is openness.

The borrowed graft: a scion received from another grower, carrying the obligation to graft onward to a stranger. The Graft Country's unit of social debt. To die with unpaid kalam udhaari is considered untidy, like leaving dishes.

The salt's memory: anything preserved in the Salt Archive, and by extension any fact kept safe from convenient forgetting. 'Put it in the salt' — said when someone tries to soften an inconvenient history.
To sow ice: the winter craft of growing artificial glaciers in the headwaters — and the watershed's general idiom for any labour whose harvest belongs entirely to people you will never meet.

The water of the unborn: the legal and spiritual status of the glaciers and aquifers under the Panj Paani Ahd. The phrase that made future generations a party to present contracts. Coined, the chronicles say, by a tired hydrologist in a tent on a dry riverbed.

Shared, held in common — the oldest and most load-bearing adjective in the watershed. Sanjha Bagh, the shared garden; sanjha chulha, the shared hearth. The political philosophy of the age compressed into two syllables.
Grapes on the barbed wire: the founding image of Sanjha Bagh, from the vine that colonised the border fence in the lawless years. Said of any sign that repair has begun without permission.

The divine order of things; the way the world actually is, as opposed to the way one would prefer it. The Faltering is remembered as the price of ignoring hukam — pumping against the truth of the water table. Engineering in this age begins with reading hukam, not overriding it.

Humility; smallness rightly sized. The governing virtue of the Glacier Trust, where every post is a rotation and nothing is built monumental. Up at the headwaters they define it operationally: nimrata is remembering that you are downstream of something too.
